Meta.startDescription: Short videos on how we build production AI, what it costs, and why over 60% of Diagnostics become Builds. Watch, then name your bottleneck.

Glossary.metaDescription: Plain-English definitions of the AI engineering terms that matter when you build a production system: evals, telemetry, RAG, inference, agents, owned infrastructure, and more.
intro: The vocabulary around production AI is noisy, and the noise hides decisions that cost real money. This glossary defines the terms PRIONATION uses when scoping and building a system — in plain language, from the point of view of a mid-market operator who has to pay for it.
Each definition is written to be useful before a Diagnostic: enough to follow a scoping conversation, challenge a vendor, and tell the difference between a feature and a liability.

terms.def: The discipline of building, shipping, and operating a production AI system — not advising on one. It covers evals, data, infrastructure, and the running service, and it ends with a system the client owns rather than a slide deck.

A repeatable test that scores an AI system's output against a defined standard: representative inputs, expected behaviour, and a scoring method. Writing the suite before the build is what makes a fixed price and a warranty honest.

A curated set of representative inputs paired with the outputs you would accept as correct. It is the reference an eval suite scores against, and the single most useful asset to assemble before any build.
Production instrumentation that records each input, output, and failure so behaviour can be measured instead of argued about. Without it, 'the model is wrong' is an opinion; with it, it is a number.

A pattern that retrieves relevant source documents at query time and gives them to the model as context, so answers are grounded in your data rather than the model's training. The standard alternative to fine-tuning for knowledge tasks.

Adapting a base model's weights by training it further on task-specific examples. It changes behaviour and style, but it is rarely the first tool to reach for — most knowledge problems are cheaper to solve with retrieval.
Designing the instructions, examples, and context handed to a model to shape its output. The cheapest lever to pull, and the first one — but not a substitute for evals or data.

A model trained to predict text, used for tasks like drafting, classifying, extracting, and answering. Powerful and probabilistic: the same input can produce different output, which is why evals matter.
Running a trained model to produce an output. Each call has a latency and a cost, so inference economics — tokens per request times request volume — decide whether a use case is viable at scale.

The maximum amount of text (measured in tokens) a model can read in a single call. It bounds how much instruction, retrieved data, and history you can supply at once, and larger is not always cheaper.
The unit of text a model reads and writes — roughly a word-piece. Pricing, context limits, and latency are all counted in tokens, so token budgets are a real engineering constraint, not a detail.
Output that is fluent and confident but factually wrong. It is a property of how language models work, not a bug to be fully removed — which is why grounding (RAG), guardrails, and evals exist.
An LLM system that plans and calls tools or actions in a loop to reach a goal, instead of answering in one shot. More capable and more failure-prone, which raises the bar on evals and telemetry.

Embeddings turn text into numbers that capture meaning; a vector database stores them so you can retrieve by similarity rather than exact match. Together they are the retrieval half of most RAG systems.

Constraints that keep model output safe, valid, and on-policy — input and output filters, schema validation, allow-lists, and fallbacks. The difference between a demo and something you can put in front of a customer.
An arrangement where the client holds the code, hosting, data, and model accounts — the opposite of vendor lock-in. It means the system keeps running, and can be changed, after the engagement ends.

principles.title: Evals before features.
principles.body: We write the test suite before we write the prompt. Production AI without evals is a demo waiting to fail.

principles.title: Telemetry from day one.
principles.body: Production data drives the next iteration. "The model is wrong" is unfalsifiable without instrumentation, so we instrument first.

principles.title: Owned infrastructure.
principles.body: You hold the keys: code, hosting, data, models. Our value is the build discipline, not infrastructure lock-in.

Tldr.label: Summary
Tldr.summary: PRIONATION.io builds production AI infrastructure for European and US-based mid-market companies (€5–50M revenue). Fixed scope. Fixed price. Lean pods. 8 weeks from signed to shipped. Starting at €5,000 for a 2-week Diagnostic.

items.q: What is AI product engineering for mid-market companies?
items.a: AI product engineering is the practice of building production AI infrastructure — not proofs-of-concept — that operates within real business workflows. For mid-market companies (€5–50M revenue), it means shipping AI products that replace manual operations within a fixed 8-week timeline, with evals defining done and clients owning all code and infrastructure.

items.q: How much does an AI build engagement cost for a €10–50M company?
items.a: PRIONATION's AI build engagements run €25,000–55,000 for an 8-week production build. The process starts with a 2-week Diagnostic at €5,000–7,000 to map the bottleneck and confirm scope. Over 60% of Diagnostics proceed to a Build. Ongoing pod access runs €4,000–9,000/month.

items.q: What does 'client owns the infrastructure' mean in practice?
items.a: At the end of every engagement, the client holds the GitHub repository, the cloud infrastructure credentials, the model provider accounts, and all telemetry data. PRIONATION delivers into the client's environment from day one. When the retainer ends, the client keeps running independently.

items.q: How is a lean pod different from hiring an AI engineer?
items.a: A 3-person PRIONATION pod costs €25,000–55,000 per project, ships in 8 weeks, and comes with evals, telemetry, and a 4-week warranty. A single AI engineer hire costs €150,000–250,000/year loaded (salary + benefits + recruitment), takes 3–6 months to ramp, and carries significant hire-quality risk. The pod is the de-risked path until internal AI engineering capability scales.

The Diagnostic is a 2-week fixed-scope engagement at €5,000–7,000. It maps the single highest-ROI operational bottleneck, defines the AI build scope, produces the eval suite specification, and delivers a production-ready architecture proposal. It is the required entry point before any Build engagement.

items.q: How does PRIONATION handle AI consulting for European companies?
items.a: PRIONATION serves EU-based mid-market companies from a remote-first structure (HQ Bali, CRO in Paris). All engagements are quoted in EUR. Client infrastructure remains within EU-compliant hosting where required. The foundation operates in English and French.
Every Build engagement includes a 4-week post-launch warranty. If production performance falls below the eval thresholds defined at the start of the build, PRIONATION fixes it at no additional cost.

Anchor.seoTitle: AI Product Engineering for Mid-Market Companies · PRIONATION.io
Anchor.metaDescription: What AI product engineering means for €5–50M companies: fixed-scope builds, owned infrastructure, and evals from day one. PRIONATION's complete guide.
Anchor.tldr: AI product engineering is the practice of building production AI infrastructure — software that runs inside real business operations — rather than slide decks or proofs-of-concept. For European and US mid-market companies (€5–50M revenue), PRIONATION ships it on a fixed scope, a fixed price, and an 8-week clock, and hands over every line of code, every credential, and all the data at the end. Engagements start with a €5,000 two-week Diagnostic.
Anchor.h1: AI product engineering for mid-market companies
intro: Most mid-market companies don't need another AI strategy deck. They need a working system that removes a specific operational bottleneck and keeps running after the consultants leave. That is what AI product engineering delivers, and it is a different discipline from advisory consulting.
This page explains what AI product engineering means for a €5–50M company, why this tier is underserved, how PRIONATION builds, and what you actually own when the engagement ends.

sections.h2: What AI product engineering actually means
body: AI product engineering treats an AI feature the way a serious software team treats any production system: it is specified, tested against measurable criteria, instrumented, deployed into the real workflow, and maintained. The deliverable is running software, not recommendations.

This is the opposite of the typical AI consulting engagement, which ends with a strategy document and a pilot that never reaches production. An advisory firm is paid for its opinion. A product engineering foundation is paid for a system that works in production, measured against criteria agreed before the build starts.

Companies between €5M and €50M in revenue sit in a gap. They are too small for the enterprise AI practices of the large consultancies, whose engagements start in the hundreds of thousands and assume a dedicated internal team. They are too complex for off-the-shelf SaaS, which solves the generic 80% and leaves the operational bottleneck — the specific 20% that actually costs money — untouched.

The result is that the highest-leverage AI work for a mid-market operator usually goes undone. PRIONATION exists to close that gap: enterprise-grade engineering practice, delivered at a scope and price a mid-market company can commit to.

Every engagement runs on four principles: evals before features (the test suite is written before the prompt), telemetry from day one (production data drives every iteration), owned infrastructure (the client holds the keys), and lean pods on fixed clocks (two to three engineers, an eight-week delivery unit). Together they are what make a fixed price honest.

The commercial path mirrors the engineering. A two-week Diagnostic maps the single highest-ROI bottleneck and defines the build scope. The eight-week Build ships the production system. An optional Retainer keeps a pod available afterwards.

AI earns its place when it removes a measurable operational constraint, not when it is interesting. In practice the bottlenecks fall into three categories: logistics and operations workflows, lead generation and qualification, and internal back-office processes that scale with headcount.

PRIONATION's selected work spans logistics operations, a logistics marketplace, and real-estate lead qualification. Each started as a specific, expensive bottleneck and ended as a running system the client owns.

sections.h2: What you own at the end
body: Control over dependency is the core principle. At handover the client holds the GitHub repository, the cloud infrastructure credentials, the model-provider accounts, and all telemetry data. PRIONATION builds inside the client's environment from day one, so there is no migration and no lock-in.

When a retainer ends, the client keeps operating the system independently. The opposite — a vendor who holds the infrastructure and rents it back — is the failure mode this principle is designed to prevent.

Engagements are quoted in euros, with fixed scope and fixed price. The Diagnostic is €5,000–7,000 over two weeks. The Build is €25,000–55,000 over eight weeks. The Retainer is €4,000–9,000 per month with a six-month minimum. A three-page Express Site is available from €1,500. Over 60% of Diagnostics proceed to a Build.

The Diagnostic is the required entry point. It is what lets PRIONATION quote a fixed price on the Build with confidence — the scope is mapped and the eval criteria are agreed before anyone commits to a number.

sections.h2: Is PRIONATION right for your company?
body: PRIONATION is a fit when you have a specific, costly operational bottleneck, leadership willing to commit to an eight-week build, and a preference for owning the result over renting it. It is not a fit if you need an open-ended research engagement or a staff-augmentation body-shop.
If you are not sure, the Diagnostic is designed to answer exactly that question in two weeks, at low cost and with no obligation to proceed.

faq.a: It is the practice of building production AI infrastructure — software that runs inside real business workflows — with the test suite written before the build, full telemetry, and the client owning all code and infrastructure. The output is a running system, not a strategy document.

faq.q: How is it different from AI consulting?
faq.a: Consulting is paid for advice and usually ends with a deck and a pilot. AI product engineering is paid for a working production system, measured against criteria agreed before the build. PRIONATION is a product engineering foundation, not an advisory firm.

A two-week, fixed-scope engagement (€5,000–7,000) that maps the highest-ROI operational bottleneck, defines the build scope, produces the eval-suite specification, and delivers a production-ready architecture proposal. It is the required entry point before any Build.
Diagnostic €5,000–7,000, Build €25,000–55,000, Retainer €4,000–9,000/month (six-month minimum), Express Site from €1,500. All quoted in euros, fixed scope and fixed price.

The client. At the end of every engagement you hold the repository, the infrastructure credentials, the model-provider accounts, and all telemetry data. PRIONATION builds inside your environment, so there is no lock-in.

faq.q: What happens after the eight weeks?
faq.a: Every Build includes a four-week post-launch warranty against the eval thresholds set at the start. After that, an optional Retainer keeps a pod available, or you operate the system entirely on your own.

pricing.label: Pricing
pricing.title: Start with a Diagnostic.
pricing.desc: Fixed scope. Fixed price. Three ways to work together.

plans.desc: We map the bottleneck. Design the architecture. Project the ROI. If the math doesn’t work, we tell you straight.
Full production AI build. Eval harness. Telemetry. Handoff documentation. 4-week warranty. You own everything.
The same engineers who built your system keep it running and evolving. Ongoing pod access.

items.q: What if the build doesn’t work?
items.a: We write evals before we write features. Every deliverable has measurable success criteria agreed upfront. If the eval suite fails, we don’t ship. The 4-week post-launch warranty covers regressions. The 8-week clock only starts after the Diagnostic scope is signed off together — no surprises.

items.q: €25–55K is a lot. Why not hire someone?
items.a: A senior AI engineer in Europe runs €120–180K loaded cost, takes 4–6 months to hire, and you get one person. Our Build is 2–3 engineers, in production in 8 weeks, for under half the first-year cost of a single hire.

8 weeks is the minimum to ship something production-grade, not a prototype. That means eval harness built, telemetry instrumented, model evaluated against real data, deployment tested, and team handed off. Faster builds skip those layers. They fail in production.

items.q: Do we own the code after?
items.a: Yes. Fully. Code, hosting, data, models — everything. We hand off the entire stack. Our value is the build discipline, not infrastructure lock-in. You can run it yourself or keep us on Retainer, but the choice is yours.

items.q: We’re already talking to other AI vendors.
items.a: Good. Ask them for their eval harness. Ask what telemetry they instrument on day one. Ask who holds the model, hosting, and data after handoff. The answers tell you everything about how they actually build.

cta.label: Connect Us
cta.title: Name your bottleneck.
cta.desc: One sentence about what’s slowing your operations down. That’s the entire qualification.
